attributes.stem:
A 64-year-old man is admitted in a moribund state secondary to perforated diverticular disease. After resuscitation he undergoes an emergency bowel resection and end-colostomy (Hartmann procedure). His medical history is provided by his wife and includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, COPD, depression and anxiety. His regular medications include metformin, empagliflozin, sertraline, and tiotropium as required for COPD. He has an extensive smoking history of 2 packets/day for nearly 40 years and currently smokes. He reports a 'few' beers.

He is making a satisfactory recovery but four days after the operation becomes aggressive, shouting and demands to be let home. He is orientated to person but not place or time. He is still requiring intravenous fluids for slow return of gut function. His vital signs from the morning are as follows: blood pressure 150/90 mmHg, pulse 120/min and regular, temperature 37.3˚C, respiratory rate 18min, SpO2 96% on room air. He has required 30mg of endone for pain in the past 24 hours, which is consistent with his previous daily requirement. He is difficult to examine further, given his current agitation, but it is noted that previously his chest has been clear, his abdomen soft non-tender with clean wound and functioning colostomy, calves soft non-tender with no erythema or oedema and no obvious rashes.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Whilst several of the other options might be correct and need to be excluded, alcohol withdrawal best fits the clinical picture. The symptoms and signs associated with alcohol withdrawal typically start three to four days after cessation of drinking. Whilst non-specific information has been given about alcohol consumption in this patient, alcohol usage disorder occurs frequently with a global prevalence of 14%. Whilst in an ideal world, patients will be asked about smoking and alcohol consumption, the reality is that this aspect of the patient's history is easy to overlook when the focus is on resuscitation and preparation for emergency surgery, as in this case. Moreover, in real clinical practice patients are often reluctant to provide an accurate alcohol history on admission.

In today's (Australian) society, it is a reasonable assumption to make that many members of the community consume alcohol on a regular basis - and that does not necessarily mean to excess. In such circumstances, sudden withdrawal from their normal routine, could produce the adverse effects as shown in this patient, including hypertension, tachycardia, confusion and agitation.

Delirium tremens is a potentially life-threatening complication of alcohol withdrawal and requires urgent management consisting of benzodiazepine administration, and thiamine and multivitamin supplementation.

Pneumonia is less likely given the previously clear chest on auscultation and lack of fever, although his COPD does predispose to this condition. Opiate toxicity is unlikely given the small quantity given over the previous 24 -hour period, which is also stable with previous use. Septicaemia is not likely given the clinical picture, with no fevers or focus of infection, and the high blood pressure and tachycardia likely explained by the delirium tremens. Hyponatraemia can also present with confusion but is associated with hypotension rather than hypertension. Sodium blood levels would be obtained in this case.
learningPoints: Acute alcohol withdrawal should always be considered in the assessment of the post-operative patient who develops confusion.
references: Grover S, Ghosh A. Delirium Tremens: Assessment and Management. J Clin Exp Hepatol. 2018 Dec;8(4):460-470. doi: 10.1016/j.jceh.2018.04.012. Epub 2018 May 5. PMID: 30564004; PMCID: PMC6286444.
Rahman A, Paul M. Delirium Tremens. 2023 Aug 14. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan–. PMID: 29489272.

Kaye AD, Staser AN, Mccollins TS, Zheng J, Berry FA, Burroughs CR, Heisler M, Mouhaffel A, Ahmadzadeh S, Kaye AM, Shekoohi S, Varrassi G. Delirium Tremens: A Review of Clinical Studies. Cureus. 2024 Apr 4;16(4):e57601. doi: 10.7759/cureus.57601. PMID: 38707114; PMCID: PMC11069634.
